title: Planked Wood Bedside Table with Shelves
summary: A farmhouse nightstand with planked 1x4 side panels, 2x2 legs, and two open shelves — brad-nailed planking over a pocket-holed frame, one table per lumber run.
description:
The 'planked' in the name is the design: instead of plywood panels, the sides are four individual 1x4 boards laid edge to edge, capped with a 1x2 trim rail — the same vocabulary as a farmhouse door. The gaps and seams aren't flaws to hide; they're the texture that makes this read as furniture with a history instead of a flat-pack box.

Structurally it's a hybrid, and the order matters: the side panels are built FLAT first (1x4s nailed to the 1x2 trim, squared on the bench), then pocket-screwed to the 2x2 legs, then the leg boxes are joined with 2x2 shelf supports pocket-holed at 1-1/2". Building panels flat on a bench is how they come out square; assembling plank-by-plank in the air is how they don't.

Two shelves of 1x4 boards drop onto the supports and nail down, and the top is five more 1x4s — planked again, seams proudly visible. If the nightstand will hold anything that can slide off the back (phones on chargers, mostly), the source's optional 1/4" plywood back is a two-minute add worth doing.

Everything here is brad-plus-glue or pocket screw — no clamps needed, no exotic joinery. The plan's cut list makes ONE table; double everything for the pair your bed actually wants.

tools.note: Both settings: 3/4" for the panels, 1-1/2" for the 2x2 shelf supports.

materials.name: 1x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Side panels, shelves, and top. Quantities for ONE table.

materials.name: 2x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs and shelf supports. Pick straight ones.

steps.title: Build the two side panels flat
steps.body:
Lay four 1x4 planks edge to edge on the bench, square them up, and nail a 1x2 trim rail across near the top and bottom — two 1-1/4" brads per plank overlap, with glue.

Check each panel for square by its diagonals BEFORE the glue sets, and build the second panel on top of the first so they match. Panels built flat come out flat; panels built in the air come out interesting.

steps.title: Attach the panels to the legs
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes on the inside edges of each panel's outer planks and screw the panels between pairs of 2x2 legs with 1-1/4" pocket screws. Keep the panel faces flush to the legs' outside faces.

steps.title: Join the sides with shelf supports
steps.body:
Set the jig to 1-1/2" and drill one pocket hole in each end of the six 2x2 shelf supports. Connect the two side assemblies — three supports per shelf level (front, back, center), 2-1/2" pocket screws and glue.

The supports set the shelf heights; put the lower shelf high enough for the books that actually live beside your bed, not the ones in the catalog photo.

steps.title: Plank the shelves
steps.body: Lay four 1x4 shelf boards across each level of supports and nail down with glue — even gaps, ends flush. The planking stiffens the frame the way decking stiffens a joist system.

steps.title: Top it, back it (maybe), finish it
steps.body:
Nail and glue the five top planks down with 1-1/4" brads, even overhangs. If phones and chargers will live here, add the optional 1/4" plywood back with 3/4" brads — the two-minute insurance against the nightly behind-the-table phone drop.

Sand well (planked surfaces have lots of edges to ease), fill brad holes or don't (farmhouse forgives), and stain or paint. Then build the second one — beds have two sides.

images.alt: Farmhouse-style planked wood nightstand with two open shelves
